content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF
GROSSFELD, DOUGHERTY & GROSSFELD
KIEFER PLAZA
SHELDON H GROSSFELD*
3201 FLORIN-PERKINS ROAD, SUI. Oct-22 10 02 AM '91 LARRY MARGOLIS
JOHN A. DOUGHERTY
P.O. BOX 276005
GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS
KENNETH B GROSSFELD
LYNN JOSEPH SCOTT
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95826-6005
DAVID C. ENGSTROM
KAREN L PATTERSON
TELEPHONE
JAMES M PIERCE
(916) 386-2700
ROCHELLE WILSON
ERIC R GARNER
FAX
LAURA A REIMCHE
(916) 386-0749
KATHRYN ALLEN
FREDRICK S. COHEN
*RETIRED JUDGE-SUPERIOR COURT

October 21, 1991


John W. Wallace, Esq.
Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Your File No. I-91-454

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Thank you for your informal assistance letter of October 17,
1991. Having conferred with you and with Jeanne Pritchard, we are
requesting herewith a formal advice letter based on the following
facts:

Our law firm is newly registered as a lobbying firm under the
Political Reform Act ("Act"). Our senior partner, Sheldon H.
Grossfeld, is a newly appointed member of the Board of
Architectural Examiners, Department of Consumer Affairs. Our
particular question is whether Mr. Grossfeld is entitled to
continue to expense to the firm the usual and customary expenses
associated with his position as a partner of the firm, including
lunches, entertainment and travel.

Please assume that the law/lobbying firm is not lobbying the
Board of Architectural Examiners or the Department of Consumer
Affairs. All expenditures by the firm for Mr. Grossfeld are in
conjunction with his position and duties as a partner of the firm
and not in conjunction with his activities as a public official.

Given those facts, is there any limitation under the Act on
the compensation Mr. Grossfeld may receive, or, conversely, on the
expenditures the firm may lawfully make on Mr. Grossfeld's behalf?

We would particularly like assurance that those payments are
not gifts within the meaning of Government Code Section 86203 and
that they are not activity expenses of the firm (as a lobbying
firm) under Government Code Section 86111. It is my belief and
understanding that such expenditures would not be reportable either

John W. Wallace, Esq.
Page 2
October 21, 1991




by the firm or by Mr. Grossfeld under the facts stated.  Is that
correct?

     Pursuant to further discussion with Ms. Pritchard, we are also
asking you for a concise statement of the limitations imposed by
law on expenditures made by the firm for entertainment of public
officials within various categories:  (1) legislators and
legislative staff persons; (2) elected and appointed state
officials; (3) elected local officials; (4) appointed local
officials; (5) members of the judiciary.

     Finally, please confirm our understanding that the $10 monthly
limit of Government Code Section 86203 applies only to legislators,
legislative staff persons, and state officials within an agency
that the particular lobbyist or lobbying firm is seeking to
influence.  In other words, if our firm is not seeking to lobby
anyone within the Department of Consumer Affairs, does the
limitation of Section 86203 apply to an expenditure by the firm for
a public official employed by that agency? It is my belief that it
does not, under the facts stated.

     Thank you for your prompt and courteous assistance to date and
for your attention to this further request.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        GROSSFELD, DOUGHERTY & GROSSFELD

                                        [signature]

                                        Margaret J. Khan

MJK:mb

Grossfeld, is a newly appointed member of the Board of Architectural Examiners, Department of Consumer Affairs. Our particular question is whether Mr. Grossfeld is entitled to continue to expense to the firm the usual and customary expenses associated with his position as a partner of the firm, including lunches, entertainment and travel. It is my belief and understanding that such expenditures would not be reportable either by the firm or by Mr. Grossfeld under the facts stated. Is that correct? Pursuant to further discussion with Ms. Pritchard, we are also asking you for a concise statement of the limitations imposed by law on expenditures made by the firm for entertainment of public officials within various categories: (1) legislators and legislative staff persons; (2) elected and appointed state officials; (3) elected local officials; (4) appointed local officials; (5) members of the judiciary. Finally, please confirm our understanding that the $10 monthly limit of Government Code Section 86203 applies only to legislators, legislative staff persons, and state officials within an agency that the particular lobbyist or lobbying firm is seeking to influence. In other words, if our firm is not seeking to lobby anyone within the Department of Consumer Affairs, does the limitation of Section 85203 apply to an expenditure by the firm for a public official employed by that agency? It is my belief that it does not, under the facts stated.

sections.question_synthetic: A newly registered lobbying firm seeks clarification on whether partner expenses (meals, entertainment, travel) for a partner who serves on a state board can be expensed to the firm without constituting gifts or lobbying activity expenses, and requests guidance on limits for entertaining various categories of public officials.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming request letter dated October 21, 1991, requesting formal FPPC advice. It references a prior informal assistance letter of October 17, 1991. The document contains detailed factual assumptions and multiple specific legal questions about gift limits and lobbying expense rules under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Our law firm is newly registered as a lobbying firm under the Political Reform Act ("Act"). Our senior partner, Sheldon H. Grossfeld, is a newly appointed member of the Board of Architectural Examiners, Department of Consumer Affairs. Our particular question is whether Mr. Grossfeld is entitled to continue to expense to the firm the usual and customary expenses associated with his position as a partner of the firm, including lunches, entertainment and travel. Please assume that the law/lobbying firm is not lobbying the Board of Architectural Examiners or the Department of Consumer Affairs. All expenditures by the firm for Mr. Grossfeld are in conjunction with his position and duties as a partner of the firm and not in conjunction with his activities as a public official. Given those facts, is there any limitation under the Act on the compensation Mr. Grossfeld may receive, or, conversely, on the expenditures the firm may lawfully make on Mr. Grossfeld's behalf? We would particularly like assurance that those payments are not gifts within the meaning of Government Code Section 86203 and that they are not activity expenses of the firm (as a lobbying firm) under Government Code Section 86111. It is my belief and understanding that such expenditures would not be reportable either by the firm or by Mr. Grossfeld under the facts stated. Is that correct? Pursuant to further discussion with Ms. Pritchard, we are also asking you for a concise statement of the limitations imposed by law on expenditures made by the firm for entertainment of public officials within various categories: (1) legislators and legislative staff persons; (2) elected and appointed state officials; (3) elected local officials; (4) appointed local officials; (5) members of the judiciary. Finally, please confirm our understanding that the $10 monthly limit of Government Code Section 86203 applies only to legislators, legislative staff persons, and state officials within an agency that the particular lobbyist or lobbying firm is seeking to influence. In other words, if our firm is not seeking to lobby anyone within the Department of Consumer Affairs, does the limitation of Section 85203 apply to an expenditure by the firm for a public official employed by that agency? It is my belief that it does not, under the facts stated.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request letter from Grossfeld, Dougherty & Grossfeld law firm requesting a formal FPPC advice letter regarding permissibility of expensing partner expenses for a senior partner who serves on the Board of Architectural Examiners while the firm conducts lobbying activities not directed at that board.
